title: Chapter 1: I Agree
content: The rain does not care about me.

That's the thing about New York storms — they're equal opportunity assholes. The thunder cracks over Manhattan like God's own whip and the rain comes down in sheets so thick you can't see the bodega across the street, and none of it gives a single damn that I'm standing in a doorway on Avenue B, crying like an idiot, with an eviction notice crumpled in my jacket pocket and exactly eleven dollars and forty-seven cents to my name.

The door behind me is locked. Pho Ky's — yes, that's really the name, and no, the irony is not lost on me — is dark inside. Dinh, the manager, flipped the open sign to closed right after he fired me. Right after he screamed at me in front of the dinner rush for dropping a plate. One plate. A fifteen-dollar plate with a goddamn lotus painted on it.
"You break it, you pay for it," he said, except I didn't have fifteen dollars, so instead he just took my last shift's wages and told me not to come back.

So here I am. Twenty-four years old, two hundred thousand dollars in student debt for a BFA that qualifies me to drop plates professionally, and the rain is hitting the pavement so hard it sounds like applause. Like the universe is clapping for my spectacular failure. Thank you, thank you, I'll be here all week — assuming my landlord doesn't literally throw me out on Thursday.

My shoes are soaked through. I can feel the cold water squishing between my toes, and I want to scream but the rain would swallow it anyway. My hair is plastered to my face and I'm tasting salt — tears or city water or both, who can tell anymore. The whole street smells like wet garbage and exhaust and that specific Manhattan perfume of ambition rotting in a gutter.
I pull out my phone.

Three percent battery. The screen is so dim in the rain I have to squint, raindrops hitting the glass and making everything look like a monet painting of my own misery. I open my bank app — not because I think anything changed, but because I'm the kind of person who checks a clock to see if time has started going backward. Maybe the student loan payment bounced. Maybe I can squeeze one more day out of eleven dollars and forty-seven cents. Maybe a Nigerian prince finally came through.
The app loads. $11.47. No mystery deposits. No errors. Just the same number staring back at me like it's disappointed too.
But there's something else on my screen.
A new app.

I didn't download a new app. I haven't downloaded anything in weeks because I've been on a 2GB data plan and also because I've been too busy getting fired from restaurants to browse the App Store. But there it is, sitting on my home screen between my bank app and the Instagram icon I keep meaning to delete:
**Mephisto MCN.**

Black icon. A stylized M that looks like flames curling upward — or maybe claws, I can't tell in this light. The design is gorgeous, actually. Looks expensive. Like something a real creative agency would make, all sharp edges and negative space.
I didn't download this.

I try to delete it. Long-press. Nothing. The other icons start wiggling like they always do — the little X buttons appear on everything else — but Mephisto just sits there. No X. No wiggle. Perfectly still on a screen full of trembling apps.
I try again. Long-press harder, like that's going to make a difference, like my thumb has any authority here. Still nothing. The icon just sits on my screen, black and gold and smug.
And then it pulses.
One beat. A soft glow that rises from the edges of the M and fades. Like a heartbeat.
My battery drops to two percent.

"Okay," I say out loud, because apparently I'm the kind of person who narrates her own meltdown now. "Okay, that's — that's a virus. That's malware. Someone hacked my phone. Great. Cool. My phone has a demon now. Of course it does."

The rain is still hammering the city. A bus rolls past on Avenue B and sends a wave of gutter water across the sidewalk, missing my shoes by approximately one inch. Even the city's aim is better than mine tonight.

I should close the app. I should power off my phone and walk the twelve blocks home through the storm and deal with whatever crypto-mining Russian malware has infected my screen tomorrow, when I have wifi and a charger and maybe the emotional bandwidth to care.

But the app is there. And my thumb is hovering over it. And honestly — what's the worst that could happen? What, exactly, is this thing going to steal? My identity? Good luck with that, my credit score is a punchline. My location? I'm standing in a doorway crying in the rain, I am not a person with valuable data.
I tap the icon.

The interface opens and it is — I have to admit this, even in my current state of psychological freefall — it is impossibly beautiful. Dark background, not black but something deeper, like the color of expensive suits and very good whiskey. Gold accents that catch the light even through the rain on my screen, scrolling in clean lines and elegant curves. Every pixel looks intentional. This wasn't made by a startup in a WeWork. This wasn't made by anyone I've ever met.
**"Mephisto MCN: Where Creators Become Legends."**

That's the welcome screen. Below it, a button that says "Begin Your Journey" in gold text that literally shimmers — not a filter, not a glitch, but an actual shimmer, like metal catching light that doesn't exist.
I tap it.
A Terms of Service document fills my screen.

I've seen Terms of Service before. Everyone has — we all scroll past them, we all click agree, we all know that somewhere in the fine print Mark Zuckerberg owns our firstborn. But this — this is something else. The scroll bar on the right side of the screen is a sliver. A hairline fracture. I drag my thumb down and the text keeps going. And going. And going.
One hundred pages.
One. Hundred. Pages.

My battery is at two percent. The rain is soaking through my jacket and into my bra and the eviction notice is pressing against my ribs like a second heartbeat. I don't have time for this. I don't have battery for this. I don't have anything — that's the whole point of tonight, the whole point of my life right now, the whole point of standing in a Pho Ky's doorway at nine PM on a Tuesday in October: I don't have anything and I don't have time.
So I scroll.

I scroll fast, thumb swiping like I'm on a TikTok binge, pages of legal text blurring past. Words jump out at me — "binding," "perpetual," "exclusive," "soul" — wait. Did that say soul? I scroll back up. No. It says "sole." Sole discretion. Sole ownership. I'm seeing things. I'm tired. I'm wet and I'm cold and I'm crying again, and the salt taste is definitely tears this time because I can feel them running down the sides of my mouth and dripping off my chin.
One percent.
A checkbox appears at the bottom. Next to it: *"I have read and agree to the Terms of Service."*

I have not read the Terms of Service. I have not even skimmed the Terms of Service. I have performed a visual acknowledgment that text existed and then scrolled past it like every other human being on earth who has ever installed an app.
I check the box.
The golden button glows.
**[I AGREE]**
My thumb hovers for half a second. Not because I'm hesitating — because my thumb is cold and the rain is making it hard to aim. I tap the button.
And the rain stops.

Not tapers. Not eases. Stops. Like someone hit pause on the entire sky. One second the world is a wall of water and the next there's nothing — no rain, no thunder, just silence. Absolute, deafening silence. The kind of quiet that makes your ears ring because they've forgotten what to do with it.

I look up. The clouds are still there, thick and black over the East Village, but they're not moving. They're not doing anything. It's like someone froze a frame of the storm and forgot to press play.
My phone screen flashes — red, bright and violent, like a warning — and then gold, warm and seductive, like sunrise over money. A notification slides down from the top:
**"Welcome to the family, Chloe. 🖤"**
My battery says one hundred percent.

I did not charge my phone. I don't even have my charger — it's at home, the one with the frayed cord that only works if you hold it at a specific angle like you're performing surgery. My phone was at one percent three seconds ago and now it's full. Not charging. Full. Like it had always been full.

I stare at the screen. The notification fades. My home screen reappears, and Mephisto MCN is there among my other apps, but it's different now. The other icons look flat, dead, pixels on glass. Mephisto's icon is — alive isn't the right word. Dimensional. The gold edges shimmer with their own light, casting the tiniest glow on the apps next to it. The M looks like it's moving, just slightly, if I'm not looking directly at it.
"You're hallucinating," I tell myself. "You're cold and wet and you're hallucinating."

Water drips from the awning above me. A taxi splashes past on Avenue B, its headlights cutting through the sudden stillness. The city is resuming — not the storm, but the city, because New York doesn't wait for anything, not even impossible silence. A woman walks past with a dripping umbrella, not looking at me. A guy on a Citi Bike swerves around a puddle. Normal. Everything is normal.
Except the rain stopped when I tapped agree, and my phone is fully charged, and there's an app on my screen that I can't delete.
I look down at my phone again. Another notification:
**"Your first content drop is scheduled for tomorrow, 6:00 AM. We've taken the liberty of optimizing your portfolio. 🚀"**
Portfolio.

I don't have a portfolio. I have a finsta with forty-seven followers — most of whom are bots or people from high school who are too polite to unfollow — where I post sad little sketches and self-deprecating captions about being a cautionary tale. My last post was a drawing of a rat eating a dollar sign with the caption "me in 5 business days." It got three likes. One was from the ratemoji account.
Optimizing my portfolio. Sure. Optimize the hell out of nothing, see what happens.

The streets are already drying. That shouldn't be possible — the rain was biblical fifteen minutes ago, and concrete doesn't absorb water that fast — but the pavement is going from flooded to damp right in front of me, like someone's ironing the city dry. I start walking. Twelve blocks home through streets that are somehow almost normal, like the storm never happened, like the sky just decided to take it all back.
My shoes are still wet. My jacket is still soaked. The eviction notice is still in my pocket. Some things an app can't fix.
Probably.

I walk past the bodega on 9th Street and consider buying a banana with the eleven dollars I have left. I don't. I walk past the laundromat where I used to do my laundry back when I had enough quarters to matter. I don't go in. I walk the twelve blocks to my apartment — fourth floor walkup, radiator that's been broken since August, a window that overlooks a brick wall — and I climb the stairs and I don't think about the app.

It's a virus. It's malware. It's some weird crypto thing. Tomorrow I'll take my fully-charged phone to the Apple Store and some Genius with a ladle haircut will fix it and I'll be back to three percent and no prospects and an eviction on Thursday. Normal. Fine. Everything is fine.

My apartment is cold. The kind of cold that has opinions — it doesn't just sit there, it presses against you, tells you things about your life choices. I don't turn on the lights. I peel off my wet jacket and drop it on the floor and leave the eviction notice crumpled in the pocket because I can't evict myself from knowledge I don't read. I kick off my shoes — still squishing, still soaked — and crawl into my bed with my clothes on because the sheets are clean and I am not.

I set my phone on my chest. Stupid habit. I know it's a stupid habit. Radiation, poor sleep posture, the whole thing — but it's warm from the battery that shouldn't be full and I'm cold from the rain that shouldn't have stopped and I need to set an alarm for tomorrow because apparently I have a content drop at six AM for a portfolio I don't have.
I set the alarm for 5:45. Close my eyes.
The Mephisto icon pulses on my home screen. Once. Soft gold light that rises and falls.
Twice.
Like breathing.

I'm already half-asleep when I notice it, and the part of me that notices is too tired to care. The M pulses — three times, four — slow and steady and patient, like it's been waiting a very long time and can wait a little longer. Like it knows exactly what it's doing.
Like it's not an app at all.

In the dark of my unheated apartment, the Mephisto icon breathes. And Chloe Hart sleeps. And somewhere in the fine print of a contract she never read, in the space between "sole" and "soul" and a hundred pages of beautiful, terrible words, something has begun that cannot be undone.
The phone glows gold in the dark.
And waits.
